# How to Get GPU Computes from ACCESS / NCSA

## 1\. Register an account

Visit: [https://identity.access-ci.org/new-user](https://identity.access-ci.org/new-user)

### Step 1: Click Register with an existing identity

![][image1]

### Step 2: Select UIUC

![][image2]

### Step 3: Following the instruction

## 2\. Start a Request

Click “Request an Explore ACCESS project”  
![][image3]  
According to the document ([https://allocations.access-ci.org/project-types](https://allocations.access-ci.org/project-types)), you can always start with an **Explore project** if you’re not sure what project to request and upgrade as you learn more about your resource needs. 

**Things you need:**

* Title of the project  
* Keywords pertaining to the research  
* Field of science  
* An overview of the research questions you intend to explore along with any details on how you intend to integrate ACCESS resources into your investigations.

* CVs for the PI and any co-PIs in PDF format.  
  * You should list yourself as PI and list your supervisor as Co-PI  
  * Ask your supervisor’s CV (only first 3 pages are needed)  
* Letter of collaboration if a Graduate Student, in PDF format.  
  * You need to submit  a signed letter of collaboration  
  * Letter of collaboration template: [DELTA\_Letter\_of\_Collab\_Template.docx](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1feIkHIfjcmrFZE3MBQTc5w925UdB-1p4FTX9agj1wKY/edit?usp=sharing)  
  * This should be submitted as *Advisor Letter*

After obtaining all these related documents, submit a request.  
NOTE: you should **not** fill in any funding project information. **This compute is only for your thesis research.**

Once you receive an email that your request has been approved, proceed to the next section.

## 3\. Exchange credits

Once you get approved, exchange your approved credit for GPU hours\!

![][image4]  
![][image5]

**Select NCSA Detla GPU in the drop-down menu, and enter the number of credits you want to exchange for GPU hours.**  
![][image6]  
Provide a short description of what you want to use these GPUs for (e.g., training large language model), then hit submit.

Then you can wait for emails from the ACCESS program to notify you about the next step (whether you are approved or not).

Typically, the ACCESS Explore program gives you 3000 GPU hours.

![][image7]  
It costed around 100k credits for 1500 Gpu hours along with 1TB of storage , It will take 2-3 days for approval, so it is better to ask for more before hand

# How to use these GPU hours

**Complete Guide:** [https://wiki.ncsa.illinois.edu/display/DSC/Delta+User+Guide](https://wiki.ncsa.illinois.edu/display/DSC/Delta+User+Guide)

## Login into the Login Node

Make sure you are connected to school wifi, otherwise, please use VPN  
[https://webstore.illinois.edu/Shop/product.aspx?zpid=2600](https://webstore.illinois.edu/Shop/product.aspx?zpid=2600)

[https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user\_guide/accessing.html\#direct-access-login-nodes](https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user_guide/accessing.html#direct-access-login-nodes)

ssh username@login.delta.ncsa.illinois.edu

## Check your quotas

### Disk Quota

| quota \# list your disk quotas  |
| :---- |

Different directly under /projects/\* are folders corresponding to your different projects.

![][image8]

#### Difference between /projects and /scratch

[https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user\_guide/architecture.html\#id2](https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user_guide/architecture.html#id2)  
![][image9]

Looks like /scratch is larger and more performant, so I recommend putting related data to it. For example, /project/bbvz/xingyao6/my\_project

#### Data Transfer

You can transfer your data under your project folder using scp or clone.

### GPU Hours Left

| accounts \# will tell you how much GPU hour is left for you to use. |
| :---- |

![][image10]

#### How do they charge GPU-Hour for different GPU types?

[https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user\_guide/running\_jobs.html\#delta-production-partitions-queues](https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user_guide/running_jobs.html#delta-production-partitions-queues)

![][image11]

**\[Charge Factor\] \* \[actual GPU hour you used\] \= \[Charged GPU hour to your account\].**

For example, If I am using gpuA100x8 with a charge factor \= 1.5 for 4 hours (actual GPU hour \= 4 \* 8 \= 32), the amount charged to my balance will be 1.5 \* 4 \* 8 \= 48 (GPU hours)

## Manage your environments

I personally prefer to use conda and docker to manage my environment. So i’ll be mainly discussing these two. Feel free to contribute if you have other ways to do it.

### Conda Environment

You can directly install conda in your home directory, which will always get mounted to the computing cluster.

You can install Mamba (a high-performance alternative of conda) and Conda via: [https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge\#mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge)

| curl \-L \-O "https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge/releases/latest/download/Miniforge3-$(uname)-$(uname \-m).sh"bash Miniforge3\-$(uname)\-$(uname \-m).sh |
| :---- |

Once installed, you can install whatever package/environment you want.

### Anaconda Environment

If you prefer using anaconda, you can also download via

| curl \-O https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/Anaconda3-\<INSTALLER\_VERSION\>-Linux-x86\_64.sh |
| :---- |

And you may find the version you want here: [https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/](https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/)  
Then install it via

| bash Anaconda3-\<INSTALLER\_VERSION\>-Linux-x86\_64.sh |
| :---- |

Note that, when install Pytorch, you don’t need to specify the cuda version, simply run “pip install torch”. You can also verify your installed packages on GPU or CUDA by starting an interactive session:

| srun \-A TODO\_YOUR\_ACCOUNT \--time=00:05:00 \--nodes=1 \--ntasks-per-node=16 \--tasks=1 \--cpus-per-task=16 \--partition=gpuA100x4 \--gpus=1 \--mem=64g \--pty /bin/bash |
| :---- |

Remember to replace “TODO\_YOUR\_ACCOUNT” with your project name, which can be found by the command “accounts”. The above example command starts an interactive session with 1 A100 for 5 minutes, and after getting allocated resources you may also verify by “nvidia-smi”:

![][image12]

### Docker Environment \-\> Apptainer

NCSA cluster does NOT support docker; however, they have an alternative to docker – apptainer. It works mostly fine if the thing you do inside docker does not require root privileges.

You can pull any docker image into apptainer format, for example:

| mkdir \-p /projects/bcbf/xingyao6/apptainer-images/cd /projects/bcbf/xingyao6/apptainer-images/apptainer pull docker://xingyaoww/pt-megatron-llm\_v1.0\# This is equivalent to docker pull xingyaoww/pt-megatron-llm\_v1.0 in docker\# It will create an image file in /projects/bcbf/xingyao6/apptainer-images/pt-megatron-llm\_v1.0.sif |
| :---- |

Once you have the image file, you may interact with your docker image (just like docker \-it):

| \#\!/bin/bashWORK\_DIR=/scratch/FOLDER\_YOU\_WANT\_TO\_MOUNTIMAGE=/projects/bcbf/xingyao6/apptainer-images/pt-megatron-llm\_v1.0.sifecho "MODEL\_DIR=$MODEL\_DIR"echo "WORK\_DIR=$WORK\_DIR"echo "IMAGE=$IMAGE"apptainer run \--nv \\    \--no-home \\    \--no-mount bind\-paths \\    \--cleanenv \\    \--env "HUGGING\_FACE\_HUB\_TOKEN=$HUGGING\_FACE\_HUB\_TOKEN" \\    \--env "WANDB\_API\_KEY=$WANDB\_API\_KEY" \\    \--writable-tmpfs \\    \--bind $WORK\_DIR:/workspace \\    $IMAGE \\    /bin/bash |
| :---- |

Inside the container, you can access your $WORK\_DIR in /workspace.  
You may also pass-in other environment variables (e.g., WANDB\_API\_KEY) like I did.

**Note**: If you don’t add (--no-home \--no-mount bind-paths \--cleanenv ), you will likely encounter some CUDA / GCC issue, since the $CC from the compute note will be carried inside the container, but the outside $CC is not binding to inside the container, hence causing issues.

## How to Start Training Job

### Slurm Script

You can create a slurm script (e.g., example.slurm):

| \#\!/bin/bash\#SBATCH \--job-name="finetune\_4xA100\_4tp"\#SBATCH \--output="logs/%j.%N.finetune\_4xA100\_4tp.out"\#SBATCH \--partition=gpuA100x4\#SBATCH \--mem=208G\#SBATCH \--nodes=1\#SBATCH \--ntasks-per-node=1  \# could be 1 for py-torch\#SBATCH \--cpus-per-task=16   \# spread out to use 1 core per numa, set to 64 if tasks is 1\#SBATCH \--constraint="scratch\&projects"\#SBATCH \--gpus-per-node=4\#SBATCH \--gpu-bind=closest   \# select a cpu close to gpu on pci bus topology\#SBATCH \--account=bcbf-delta-gpu\#SBATCH \--exclusive  \# dedicated node for this job\#SBATCH \--no-requeue\#SBATCH \-t 16:00:00module reset \# drop modules and explicitly load the ones needed             \# (good job metadata and reproducibility)             \# $WORK and $SCRATCH are now setmodule list  \# job documentation and metadataecho "job is starting on \`hostname\`"\# run the container binary with arguments: python3 \<program.py\>\# \--bind /projects/bbXX  \# add to apptainer arguments to mount directory inside containerMODEL\_DIR=/projects/bcbf/xingyao6/modelsWORK\_DIR=/scratch/bcbf/xingyao6/llm-agentIMAGE=/projects/bcbf/xingyao6/apptainer-images/pt-megatron-llm\_v1.0.sifecho "MODEL\_DIR=$MODEL\_DIR"echo "WORK\_DIR=$WORK\_DIR"echo "IMAGE=$IMAGE"apptainer run \--nv \\    \--no-home \\    \--no-mount bind\-paths \\    \--cleanenv \\    \--env "HUGGING\_FACE\_HUB\_TOKEN=$HUGGING\_FACE\_HUB\_TOKEN" \\    \--env "WANDB\_API\_KEY=$WANDB\_API\_KEY" \\    \--writable-tmpfs \\    \--bind $WORK\_DIR:/workspace \\    \--bind $MODEL\_DIR:/models \\    $IMAGE \\    /bin/bash \-c "cd /workspace && scripts/slurm/configs/finetune\_4xA100\_4tp.sh"\# sbatch scripts/slurm/configs/finetune\_4xA100\_4tp.slurm\# squeue \-u $USER |
| :---- |

The top part is the slurm parameters, which you can read more here: [https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user\_guide/running\_jobs.html\#](https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user_guide/running_jobs.html#)

Please replace \--account=bcbf-delta-gpu to \[YOUR PROJECT ID\]-delta-gpu, and you can find this by the “accounts” command.  
Be sure to specify the max time (\#SBATCH \-t Hour:Minute:Second) you expect this job to have. This will be used to determine the job scheduling order.

In this example script, I use apptainer to run a script \`scripts/slurm/configs/finetune\_4xA100\_4tp.sh\` within the docker image. You do not necessarily need to use apptainer; you can also run whatever you want with conda environment to replace the apptainer command.

You may find more example scripts here: [https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user\_guide/running\_jobs.html\#sample-scripts](https://ncsa-delta-doc.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/user_guide/running_jobs.html#sample-scripts)

### Slurm Script (without using Docker and Apptainer)

### Start training

Just do:

| sbatch example.slurm |
| :---- |

It will schedule your job to the cluster.

You can check status by:

| squeue \-u $USER |
| :---- |

![][image13]

## Launching Jupyter notebook in interactive sessions

| srun \--account=wxyz-delta-gpu \--partition=gpuA100x4\-interactive \\  \--time=00:30:00 \--mem=64g \--gpus-per-node=1 \\  singularity run \--nv \--bind /projects/bbka \\  /sw/external/NGC/pytorch:22.02\-py3 jupyter-notebook \\  \--notebook-dir /projects/wxyz \\  \--no-browser \--port=8991 \--ip=0.0.0.0 |
| :---- |

## GUI version of DELTA 

Url : [openondemand.delta.ncsa.illinois.edu](http://openondemand.delta.ncsa.illinois.edu)

![][image14]  
Login using your NCSA credentials  
![][image15]  
This GUI contains Files, Interactive apps like jupyter lab and you can monitor job status.

![][image16]  
You can select the required parameters and  launch the lab. I later observed that we cant add some extra parameters through the GUI which are available from CLI but we launch a shell from here and work on it.  
![][image17]  
You can access all folder structures from the GUI

## VS Code

If you want to connect to do coding, you can consider using VS Code,

1. Install “Remote \- SSH” extension

	![][image18]

2. Then go to your local $HOME/.ssh/config to finish the configuration. In VS code, you can click the blue button on the bottom-left corner (“Open a Remote Window”), then select “Connect to Host”, and next select “Configure SSH Hosts”, it will prompt you the config file to choose:

	![][image19]  
	Then select “.../.ssh/config”

3. Here is an example entry for Delta, change your username to your login name on Delta. Visual Studio will show hosts in your config in a pick list.

| Host delta         HostName login.delta.ncsa.illinois.edu         User yubinge2         ForwardX11 True |
| :---- |

4. After the configuration, you can click the blue button on the bottom-left corner again, then select “Connect to Host” \-\> “delta”, then just follow the prompt to login.
